html:
Nayax is not the $23 million microcap implied by the stale input. The TASE share is quoted in agorot. At the latest available May 15 close, TASE:NYAX was 18,630 agorot, or ILS 186.30, while Nasdaq:NYAX closed at $63.39. Real equity value is roughly $2.4 billion, with about 37.3 million basic shares outstanding, not $23 million. That kills the prior negative enterprise value thesis, but it does not kill the stock thesis. It changes it into something cleaner: Nayax is a scarce, still-underfollowed public payments compounder whose current valuation only works if the 2026 to 2028 margin transition is real.

The business is often described as vending payments, which undersells it. Nayax is becoming the operating system and payment rail for unattended commerce: vending, micro-markets, car washes, amusement, laundromats, EV charging, kiosks, and other self-service sites where the merchant needs hardware, telemetry, payment acceptance, settlement, loyalty, inventory software, and remote management in one stack. The hardware is not the profit pool. Hardware places the node. The value is in the recurring SaaS fee and the payment take-rate that run through that node for years.

The latest numbers show the transition. In Q1 2026, revenue grew 32% to $106.9 million, organic revenue grew 26%, recurring revenue grew 27% to $79.3 million and reached 74% of revenue, total transaction value grew 33% to $1.8 billion, connected devices reached 1.5 million, customers reached 120,000, and ARPU grew 14% to $247. Processing gross margin improved to 39.8% and SaaS gross margin was 76.5%. That is the central fact: this is no longer a hardware reseller with a payment attachment. It is a recurring payments and software platform with a large installed base and operating leverage starting to show.

The stock is not obviously cheap on 2026 numbers. Management reaffirmed 2026 guidance for $510 million to $520 million of revenue, $85 million to $90 million of adjusted EBITDA, and free cash flow conversion of roughly 40% of adjusted EBITDA. At $63.39, basic enterprise value is around $2.45 billion, so the stock is around 4.8x 2026 revenue and 28x 2026 adjusted EBITDA. That looks expensive if Nayax is a 15% margin payments hardware hybrid. It looks cheap if Nayax is on the way to the 2028 framework management keeps repeating: $1 billion of revenue, 50% gross margin, and 30% adjusted EBITDA margin. On that outcome, today’s enterprise value is only about 8x 2028 adjusted EBITDA.

The variant perception is that the complexity is hiding the inflection. The financials have settlement cash, restricted cash, payables tied to processing activity, TASE notes, warrants, FX, acquisition accounting, one-time gains, and lumpy free cash flow. Q1 free cash flow was negative $6 million, but the company attributed it to infrastructure investment and settlement timing; FY 2025 operating cash flow was $40.3 million and free cash flow was $12.2 million despite heavy investment. The balance sheet is not distressed: Q1 cash and short-term deposits were $306.2 million versus $325.3 million of debt.

The EV charging angle matters because it can raise ARPU and extend Nayax beyond legacy vending. The Lynkwell acquisition cost $25.9 million in cash for a business with audited 2024 revenue of $17.1 million. That is not a reckless moonshot; it is a tuck-in that gives Nayax charge-point management software next to its card-present payment stack. Since then, Nayax announced an E-Plug partnership covering an existing network of about 2,200 connections with expected future charger deployments, and a Tritium partnership across more than 50 countries. EV charging is messy, but the need for open-loop, card-present payments is real as regulators and customers push networks away from app-only payment experiences.

The best comp just disappeared into private hands. 365 Retail Markets completed its $848 million acquisition of Cantaloupe in May 2026, paying $11.20 per share for a slower-growing unattended retail technology and payments peer. Cantaloupe had guided FY 2025 revenue to $322 million to $328 million and adjusted EBITDA to $46 million to $50 million, implying about 2.6x revenue and 17x to 18x adjusted EBITDA. Nayax deserves a premium to that if it can sustain 20% plus organic growth, expand processing margins through smart routing and acquiring contracts, and keep ARPU rising through higher-ticket verticals.

The retail and operator context is mixed in the useful way. Reddit vending threads show real usage, onboarding questions, reimbursement complaints, and customer-service frustration, including direct comparisons with Cantaloupe. That is a real risk: if service quality does not scale, the long-tail merchant base will complain and some will switch. But the official operating metrics point the other way so far: FY 2025 dollar-based net retention was 120% and churn was only 2.8%. The product is embedded enough to irritate users when it fails, which is exactly what payment infrastructure looks like before it is modeled as infrastructure.

The main risk is paying too early for the 2028 model. Nayax has in-the-money TASE warrants expiring in 2027, debt raised at 5.9%, ongoing acquisition appetite, and a free cash flow profile that is still not smooth. If the company has to buy too much revenue to reach $1 billion, the 2028 target may create dilution or integration drag rather than operating leverage. If EV charging hardware and software remain a fragmented, subsidy-driven market, Lynkwell may not carry the strategic value management expects. And if Q1’s negative free cash flow is not just timing, the market will stop valuing adjusted EBITDA generously.

Base case: Nayax reaches $700 million to $800 million of revenue by 2028, margins expand into the low 20s rather than the full 30%, and the market values the company at roughly 19x to 20x forward adjusted EBITDA because recurring revenue remains above 70% and organic growth stays above 18%. That supports about $85 per Nasdaq share, or roughly 25,000 agorot on TASE, using a fully diluted share count in the low 40 millions. From $63.39 and 18,630 agorot, that is about 34% upside.

Bull case: management gets close to the 2028 framework: $1 billion of revenue, about $300 million of adjusted EBITDA, continued 50% gross margin, strong EV/software contribution, and no major dilution beyond the existing warrant overhang. A 16x to 17x EBITDA multiple on a scarce global unattended payments platform supports about $125 per Nasdaq share, or roughly 36,700 agorot on TASE. That is about 97% upside.

Bear case: 2026 guidance proves close to peak optimism, free cash flow remains lumpy, customer support issues pressure churn, EV charging adds complexity without margin, and the market values Nayax closer to Cantaloupe’s takeout multiple despite faster growth. At 16x to 17x 2026 adjusted EBITDA, the stock is worth about $40 per Nasdaq share, or roughly 11,800 agorot on TASE. That is about 37% downside.

The stock is no longer a hidden balance-sheet special situation. It is a forward multiple compression bet in reverse: expensive on current earnings, potentially cheap on the platform Nayax is visibly becoming. The upside case is not that investors discover cash. It is that investors stop treating the business as vending hardware and start valuing it as a global recurring payments and commerce software network.
